1046.text: (2) if no such date or rate is specified, to be paid immediately.

1048.text: If the claimant does not accept the amount offered by the defendant, he may obtain judgment by filing a request in Form P15/03.

1050.text: Judgment under Rule 15.30 will be for an amount to be decided by the Court and costs.

1051.text: Directions In Relation To Outstanding Matters

1053.text: Where the Court enters judgment under Rules 15.23 or 15.30 for an amount to be decided by the Court, it will give any directions it considers appropriate.

1056.text: A defendant who makes an admission under Rules 15.14, 15.18 or 15.24 (admission relating to a claim for a specified amount of money or offering to pay a specified amount of money) may make a request for time to pay.

1058.text: A request for time to pay is a proposal about the date of payment or a proposal to pay by instalments at the times and rate specified in the request.

1060.text: If the defendant is requesting time to pay he should complete as fully as possible the statement of means contained in the admission form, or otherwise give in writing the same details of his means as could have been given in the admission form.

1062.text: If the claimant accepts the defendant’s request, he may obtain judgment by filing a request in Form P13/01.

1064.text: On receipt of the request for judgment, the Court will enter judgment:

1065.text: (1) where Rule 15.14 applies, for the amount of the claim (less any payments made) and costs;

1066.text: (2) where Rule 15.18 applies, for the amount admitted (less any payments made) and costs; or

1067.text: (3) where Rule 15.24 applies, for the amount offered by the defendant (less any payments made) and

1069.text: (4) (in all cases) for payment at the time and rate specified in the defendant’s request for time to pay.

1072.text: Where the defendant has made a request for time to pay under Rule 15.33 if the claimant does not accept the defendant’s proposals for payment, he must file a notice in Form P13/01.

1074.text: Where the defendant’s admission was served direct on the claimant , a copy of the admission and the request for time to pay must be filed with the claimant’s notice.

1076.text: When the Court receives the claimant’s notice, it will enter judgment for the amount admitted (less any payments made) to be paid at the time and rate of payment determined by the Registrar.

1078.text: Where the Registrar is to determine the time and rate of payment, he may do so without a hearing.

1080.text: If there is to be a hearing to determine the time and rate of payment, the Court must give each party at least 7 days’ notice of the hearing.

1082.text: Where the Registrar has determined the time and rate of payment under Rule 15.42, either party may apply for the decision to be re-determined by a Judge.

1084.text: An application for re-determination must be made within 14 days after service of the determination on the applicant.

1086.text: In deciding the time and rate of payment the Court will take into account:

1087.text: (1) the defendant’s statement of means set out in the admission form or in any other written notice of the admission filed,

1088.text: (2) the claimant’s objections to the defendant’s request set out in the claimant’s notice, and

1091.text: Either party may, on account of a change in circumstances since the date of the decision (or redetermination as the case may be) apply to vary the time and rate of payment of instalments still remaining unpaid.

1093.text: An application to vary under Rule 15.47 above should be made in accordance with Part 23.

1096.text: Judgment under Rule 15.14 (admission of whole of claim for specified amount of money) shall include the amount of interest claimed to the date of judgment if:

1097.text:
(1) the claim form includes the details required by Rule 17.18;   
(2) where interest is claimed under (a) Articles 118 or 119 of the Contract Law 2004; (b) Articles 17, 18 or 32 of the Law of Damages and Remedies 2005; (c) Article 121(b) of the Law of Obligations 2005; or (d) Article 80 of the DIFC Employment Law 2005;

1098.text: the rate is no higher than the rate of interest fixed by these Rules under Article 39(2)(a) of the Court Law payable on judgment debts at the date when the claim form was issued; and

1099.text: (3) the claimant’s request for judgment includes a calculation of the interest claimed for the period from the date up to which interest was stated to be calculated in the claim form to the date of the request for judgment.

1101.text: In any case where judgment is entered under Rule 15.14 and the conditions in Rule 15.49 are not satisfied judgment shall be for an amount of interest to be decided by the Court.

1103.text: Where judgment is entered for an amount of interest to be decided by the Court, the Court will give directions for the management of the case.

1106.text: An admission made under this Part may be withdrawn with the Court’s permission.

1108.text: In deciding whether to give permission for an admission to be withdrawn, the Court will have regard to all the circumstances of the case, including:

1109.text: (1) the grounds upon which the applicant seeks to withdraw the admission including whether or not new evidence has come to light which was not available at the time the admission was made;

1110.text: (2) the conduct of the parties, including any conduct which led the party making the admission to do so;

1111.text: (3) the prejudice that may be caused to any person if the admission is withdrawn;

1112.text: (4) the prejudice that may be caused to any person if the application is refused;

1113.text: (5) the stage in the proceedings at which the application to withdraw is made, in particular in relation to the date or period fixed for trial;

1114.text: (6) the prospects of success (if the admission is withdrawn) of the claim or part of the claim in relation to which the offer was made; and

1115.text: (7) the interests of the administration of justice.

1121.text: This Part does not apply where the claimant uses the procedure set out in Part 8 (alternative procedure for claims).

1123.text: In relation to specialist proceedings in respect of which special provisions for defence and reply are made by these Rules and Practice Directions applicable to those claims, the provisions of this Part apply only to the extent that they are not inconsistent with those Rules and Practice Directions.

1126.text: A defendant who wishes to defend all or part of a claim must file a defence.

1127.text: Consequence Of Not Filing A Defence

1128.text: 16.4 If a defendant fails to file a defence, the claimant may obtain default judgment if Part 13 allows it.

1130.text: 16.5 A party may file a defence by submitting it electronically using the Court ’s e-filing system.

1132.text: 16.6 Part 22 requires a defence to be verified by a statement of truth.

1134.text: The form of the statement of truth is as follows:

1135.text: ‘[I believe][the defendant believes] that the facts stated in this defence are true.’

1137.text: Attention is drawn to Section VI of Part 29 which sets out the consequences of verifying a statement of case containing a false statement without an honest belief in its truth.

1138.text: The Period For Filing A Defence

1140.text: The general rule is that the period for filing a defence is:

1141.text:
(1) 14 days after service of the particulars of claim; or   
(2) if the defendant files an acknowledgment of service under Part 11, 28 days after service of the particulars of claim.

1143.text: The general rule is subject to the following rules:

1144.text: (1) Rule 9.57 (which specifies how the period for filing a defence is calculated where the claim form is served out of the jurisdiction );

1145.text: (2) Rule 12.10 (which provides that, where the defendant makes an application disputing the Court’s

1146.text: jurisdiction , he need not file a defence before the hearing);

1147.text: (3) Rule 24.5 (which provides that, if the claimant applies for immediate judgment before the defendant has filed a defence, the defendant need not file a defence before the immediate judgment hearing); and

1148.text: (4) Rule 9.48 (which requires the Court to specify the period for responding to the particulars of claim when it makes an order under Rule 9.45).

1149.text: Agreement Extending The Period For Filing A Defence

1151.text: The defendant and the claimant may agree that the period for filing a defence specified in Rules 16.9 and 16.10 shall be extended by up to 28 days.

1153.text: Where the defendant and the claimant agree to extend the period for filing a defence, the defendant must notify the Court in writing.

1156.text: A copy of the defence must be served on every other party.

1158.text: 16.14 Part 21 applies to a defendant who wishes to make a counterclaim .

1160.text: Where a defendant to a claim serves a counterclaim under Part 21, the defence and counterclaim should normally form one document, with the counterclaim following on from the defence.

1163.text: If a claimant files a reply to the defence, he must:

1164.text: (1) file his reply within 21 days after service of the defence; and

1165.text: (2) serve it on all other parties at the same time.

1167.text: The reply should be served before case management information sheets are provided to the Court . In the normal case, this will allow the parties to consider any reply before completing the case management

1168.text: information sheet, and allow time for the preparation of the case memorandum and the list of issues each of which is required for the case management conference.

1170.text: In some cases, more than 21 days may be needed for the preparation, service and filing of a reply. In such cases an application should be made on paper for an extension of time and for a postponement of the case management conference.

1172.text: Where a claimant serves a reply and a defence to counterclaim , the reply and defence to counterclaim should normally form one document, with the defence to counterclaim following on from the reply.

1173.text: No Statement Of Case After A Reply To Be Filed Without Court’S Permission

1175.text: A party may not file or serve any statement of case after a reply without the permission of the Court .

1176.text: Claimant’S Notice Where Defence Is That Money Claimed Has Been Paid

1179.text: (1) the only claim (apart from a claim for costs and interest) is for a specified amount of money; and

1180.text: (2) the defendant states in his defence that he has paid to the claimant the amount claimed,

1181.text: the Court will send notice to the claimant requiring him to state in writing whether he wishes the proceedings to continue.

1183.text: The claimant must respond to a notice issued by the Court under Rule 16.21 within 28 days after service of the notice on him. When the claimant responds, he must serve a copy of his response on the defendant .

1185.text: If the claimant fails to respond under Rule 16.22 within 28 days after service of the Court’s notice on him, the claim shall be stayed .

1187.text: Where a claim is stayed under this Rule, any party may apply for the stay to be lifted.

1188.text: Claim Stayed If It Is Not Defended Or Admitted

1191.text: (1) at least 6 months have expired since the end of the period for filing a defence specified in Rules 16.9 and 16.10;

1192.text: (2) no defendant has served or filed an admission or filed a defence or counterclaim ; and

1193.text: (3) the claimant has not entered or applied for judgment under Part 13 (default judgment ), or Part 24 (immediate judgment);

1196.text: Where a claim is stayed under Rule 16.25, any party may apply for the stay to be lifted.

1199.text: Any application made under Rule 16.24 or Rule 16.26 should be made in accordance with Part 23 and should give the reason for the applicant’s delay in proceeding with or responding to the claim.

1204.text: This Part does not apply where the claimant uses the procedure set out in Part 8 (alternative procedure for claims).

1206.text: Where special provisions about statements of case are made by the Rules and Practice Directions applying to particular types of proceedings, the provisions of this Part apply only to the extent that they are not inconsistent with those Rules and Practice Directions.

1208.text: The Court may at any time before or after the issue of the claim form order a claim to proceed without the filing or service of statements of case.

1211.text: The claim form must be headed with the title of the proceedings, including the full name of each party. The full name means, in each case where it is known:

1212.text: (1) in the case of an individual, his full unabbreviated name and title by which he is known;

1213.text: (2) in the case of an individual carrying on business in a name other than his own name, the full unabbreviated name of the individual, together with the title by which he is known, and the full trading name;

1214.text: (3) in the case of a partnership (other than a limited liability partnership (LLP))—

1215.text: (a) where partners are being sued in the name of the partnership, the full name by which the partnership is known, together with the words $' ( \mathsf { A F i r m } ) ^ { \prime }$ ; or

1216.text: (b) where partners are being sued as individuals, the full unabbreviated name of each partner and the title by which he is known;

1217.text: (4) in the case of a company or limited liability partnership registered in the DIFC, the full registered name, including suffix (LLC, limited, LLP, etc), if any;

1218.text: (5) in the case of any other company or corporation, the full name by which it is known, including suffix where appropriate.

1219.text: Claimant’S Address To Be Included In The Claim Form

1221.text: The claim form must include the claimant’s mailing address or an address at which the claimant resides or carries on business. This Rule applies even though the claimant’s address for service is the business address of his legal representative .

1223.text: Where the defendant is an individual, the claimant should (if he is able to do so) include in the claim form the defendant’s mailing address or an address at which the defendant resides or carries on business. This Rule applies even though the defendant’s legal representative has agreed to accept service on the defendant’s behalf.

1225.text: If the claim form does not show an address as required by Rule 17.5, the claim form will not be issued. The Court will notify the claimant .

1231.text: In proceedings against the Government , the Centre Authority or any of the Centre’s Bodies, the claim form must comply with the requirements of Part 41.

1233.text: If the particulars of claim are not contained in, or are not served with the claim form, the claimant must state on the claim form that the particulars of claim will follow if an acknowledgement of service is filed which indicates an intention to defend the claim.

1235.text: If the claimant is claiming in a representative capacity, the claim form must state what that capacity is.

1237.text: If the defendant is sued in a representative capacity, the claim form must state what that capacity is.

1239.text: The Court may grant any remedy to which the claimant is entitled even if that remedy is not specified in the claim form.

1240.text: Contents Of The Particulars Of Claim — General

1242.text: A claimant is not required to serve particulars of claim unless and until the defendant serves an acknowledgment of service in response to the claim form.

1244.text: Where the claimant does not include the particulars of claim in the claim form, particulars of claim may be served separately:

1245.text: (1) either at the same time as the claim form, or (2) within 28 days of the filing of an acknowledgment of service which indicates an intention to defend.

1247.text: Particulars of claim served separately from the claim form must also contain:

1248.text:
(1) [deleted],   
(2) the claim number,   
(3) the title of the proceedings, and (4) the claimant’s address for service .

1253.text: If the claimant is seeking interest he must:

1254.text: (1) state whether he is doing so:

1255.text: (a) under the terms of a contract; (b) under an enactment and if so which; or (c) on some other basis and if so what that basis is; and (2) if the claim is for a specified amount of money, state:

1257.text: Contents Of The Particulars Of Claim — Specific

1259.text: Where a claim is made for an injunction or declaration in respect of or relating to any land the particulars of claim must:

1260.text: (1) state whether or not the injunction or declaration relates to residential premises, and (2) identify the land (by reference to a plan where necessary).

1262.text: Where a claim is brought to enforce a right to recover possession of goods the particulars of claim must contain a statement showing the value of the goods.

1264.text: Where a claim is brought for personal injury:

1265.text: (1) the particulars of claim must contain:

1266.text: (a) the claimant’s date of birth, and

1267.text: (b) brief details of the claimant’s personal injuries;

1268.text: (2) the claimant must attach to his particulars of claim a schedule of details of any past and future expenses and losses which he claims; and

1269.text: (3) where the claimant is relying on the evidence of a medical practitioner the claimant must attach to or serve with his particulars of claim a report from a medical practitioner about the personal injuries which he alleges in his claim.

1270.text: Matters Which Must Be Specifically Set Out In The Particulars Of Claim If Relied On

1272.text: A claimant who wishes to rely on:

1273.text: (1) a Certificate of Contravention under Article 55 of the Markets Law 2004; or (2) a Certificate setting out a decision of the Financial Markets Tribunal under Article 34(6) of the Regulatory Law 2004; must include in his particulars of claim a statement to that effect and give the following details:

1274.text:
(a) the type of finding and its date;   
(b) the issue in the claim to which it relates.

1277.text: If the defendant has not filed an acknowledgment of service under Part 11, he must give an address for service .

1279.text: Where the defendant is an individual, and the claim form does not contain an address for the defendant in accordance with Rule 17.6, or contains an incorrect address, the defendant must provide such an address in the defence.

1281.text: Where the defendant’s address for service is not the defendant’s mailing address or the address where he resides or carries on business, the defendant must still provide the address required by Rule 17.24.

1283.text: In his defence, the defendant must state:

1284.text:
(1) which of the allegations in the particulars of claim he denies;   
(2) which allegations he is unable to admit or deny, but which he requires the claimant to prove; and (3) which allegations he admits.

1286.text: Where the defendant denies an allegation:

1287.text:
(1) he must state his reasons for doing so; and   
(2) if he intends to put forward a different version of events from that given by the claimant , he must state his own version.

1290.text: (1) fails to deal with an allegation; but (2) has set out in his defence the nature of his case in relation to the issue to which that allegation is relevant, shall be taken to require that allegation to be proved.

1292.text: Where the claim includes a money claim, a defendant shall be taken to require that any allegation relating to the amount of money claimed be proved unless he expressly admits the allegation.

1294.text: Subject to Rules 17.28 and 17.29, a defendant who fails to deal with an allegation shall be taken to admit that allegation.

1296.text: If the defendant is defending in a representative capacity, he must state what that capacity is.

1298.text: The defendant must give details of the expiry of any relevant limitation period relied on.

1302.text: (1) contends he is entitled to money from the claimant ; and (2) relies on this as a defence to the whole or part of the claim,

1303.text: the contention may be included in the defence and set off against the claim, whether or not it is also a counterclaim .

1306.text: A claimant who does not file a reply to the defence shall not be taken to admit the matters raised in the defence.

1309.text: (1) files a reply to a defence; but

1310.text: (2) fails to deal with a matter raised in the defence,

1311.text: shall be taken to require that matter to be proved.

1312.text: General Rules About Statements Of Case

1314.text: Particulars of claim, the defence and any reply must be set out in separate consecutively numbered paragraphs and be as brief and concise as possible.

1317.text: (1) refer in his statement of case to any point of law on which his claim or defence, as the case may be, is based;

1318.text: (2) give in his statement of case the name of any witness he proposes to call; and

1319.text: (3) serve with his statement of case a copy of any document which he considers is necessary to his claim or defence, as the case may be.

1321.text: If it is necessary for the proper understanding of the statement of case to include substantial parts of a lengthy document the passages in question may be set out in a schedule rather than in the body of the statement of case .

1323.text: Only the relevant parts of those documents which are obviously of critical importance and necessary for a proper understanding of the statement of case should be attached to or served with it. The statement of case must itself refer to the fact that documents are attached to or served with it.

1325.text: If documents are to be served at the same time as a statement of case they should normally be served separately from rather than attached to the statement of case .

1327.text: Where a party relies on an oral agreement, the statement of case should set out the contractual words used and state by whom, to whom, when and where they were spoken.

1329.text: Where a party relies on an agreement by conduct, the statement of case must specify the conduct relied on and state by whom, when and where the acts constituting the conduct were done.

1331.text: A party must specifically set out the following matters in his statement of case where he wishes to rely on them in support of his case:

1332.text: (1) full and specific details of any allegation of fraud, dishonesty, malice or illegality;

1334.text: (3) details of all breaches of trust;

1335.text: (4) notice or knowledge of a fact;

1336.text: (5) details of unsoundness of mind or undue influence;

1338.text: (7) any facts relating to mitigation of loss or damage.

1340.text: Any legislative provision on which an allegation is based must be clearly identified and the basis of its application explained.

1342.text: Any principle of foreign law or foreign legislative provision on which a party’s case is based must be clearly identified and the basis of its application explained.

1344.text: An expert’s report should not be attached to the statement of case and should not be filed with the statement of case at the Registry. A party must obtain permission from the Court in order to adduce expert evidence at trial and therefore any party which serves an expert’s report without obtaining such permission does so at his own risk as to costs.

1346.text: If a statement of case exceeds 25 pages (excluding schedules), a summary, not exceeding 4 pages, must also be filed and served at the same time as the statement of case . The summary should cross-refer to the paragraph numbering of the full statement of case . The summary is to be included in the case management bundle.

1348.text: A statement of case must be signed by the individual person(s) who drafted it or bear the name of the lawyer by whom it was settled.

1350.text: A statement of case must be verified by a statement of truth, the form of which is as follows:

1351.text: [I believe][the claimant/defendant believes] that the facts stated in this claim form/these particulars of claim/this defence [and counterclaim/this reply and defence to counterclaim] are true.’

1353.text: A subsequent statement of case must not contradict or be inconsistent with an earlier one; for example a reply to a defence must not bring in a new claim. Where new matters have come to light the appropriate course is to seek the Court’s permission to amend the statement of case .

1354.text: PART 18 Amendments To Statements Of Case

1357.text: 18.1 A party may amend his statement of case at any time before it has been served on any other party.

1359.text: If his statement of case has been served, a party may amend it only:

1360.text: (1) with the written consent of all the other parties; or (2) with the permission of the Court.

1362.text: If a statement of case has been served, an application to amend it by removing, adding or substituting a party must be made in accordance with Part 20.

1363.text: Power Of Court To Disallow Amendments Made Without Permission

1364.text:
18.4   
If a party has amended his statement of case where permission of the Court was not required, the Court may disallow the amendment.

1366.text: A party may apply to the Court for an order under Rule 18.4 within 14 days of service of a copy of the amended statement of case on him.

1367.text: Amendments To Statements Of Case With The Permission Of The Court

1369.text: Where the Court gives permission for a party to amend his statement of case , it may give directions as to:

1370.text: (1) amendments to be made to any other statement of case ; and (2) service of any amended statement of case .

1372.text: The power of the Court to give permission under Rule 18.6 is subject to:

1374.text: Amendments To Statements Of Case After The End Of A Relevant Limitation Period

1377.text: (1) a party applies to amend his statement of case in one of the ways mentioned in Rule 18; and (2) a period of limitation has expired under:

1380.text: The Court may allow an amendment whose effect will be to add or substitute a new claim, but only if the new claim arises out of the same facts or substantially the same facts as a claim in respect of which the party applying for permission has already claimed a remedy in the proceedings.

1382.text: The Court may allow an amendment to correct a mistake as to the name of a party, but only where the mistake was genuine and not one which would cause reasonable doubt as to the identity of the party in question.

1384.text: The Court may allow an amendment to alter the capacity in which a party claims if the new capacity is one which that party had when the proceedings started or has since acquired.

1385.text: Applications To Amend Where The Permission Of The Court Is Required

1387.text: Questions of amendment, and consequential amendment, should wherever possible be dealt with by consent. A party should consent to a proposed amendment unless he has substantial grounds for objecting to it.

1389.text: The application may be dealt with at a hearing or, if Rule 23.68 applies, without a hearing.

1391.text: When making an application to amend a statement of case , the applicant should file with the Court:

1392.text: (1) the application notice ; (2) a copy of the statement of case with the proposed amendments; and (3) any evidence submitted in support of the application.

1394.text: Late amendments should be avoided and may be disallowed.

1397.text: Where permission to amend has been given, the applicant should within 14 days of the date of the order, or within such other period as the Court may direct, file with the Court the amended statement of case .

1399.text: A copy of the order and the amended statement of case should be served on every party to the proceedings, unless the Court orders otherwise.

1401.text: Where the Court’s permission is not required the amended statement of case must be filed with the Court and served on every party to the proceedings.

1403.text: The amended statement of case and the Court copy of it should be endorsed as follows:

1404.text: (1) where the Court’s permission was required:

1405.text: “Amended [Particulars of Claim or as may be] by Order of [The Honourable Chief Justice, the Honourable Mr. Justice X, Mr. Registrar X, Deputy Registrar X or as may be] dated……..;

1406.text: (2) Where the Court’s permission was not required:

1407.text: Amended [Particulars of Claim or as may be] under RDC [Rule 18.1 or 18.2(1)] dated…….

1409.text: The statement of case in its amended form must show the original text, unless the Court orders otherwise.

1411.text: Amendments may be shown by using footnotes or marginal notes, provided they identify precisely where and when an amendment has been made.

1413.text: However, the Court may direct that the amendments should be shown either:

1414.text: (1) by coloured amendments, either manuscript or computer generated, or

1415.text: (2) by use of a numerical code in a monochrome computer generated document.

1417.text: Where colour is used, the text to be deleted should be struck through in colour and any text replacing it should be inserted or underlined in the same colour.

1419.text: The order of colours to be used for successive amendments is: (1) red, (2) green, (3) blue and (4) brown.

1421.text: If there have been extensive amendments, it may be desirable to prepare a fresh version of the statement of case as amended. However, a copy of the statement of case showing where and when amendments have been made must also be made available.

1423.text: All amendments to any statement of case must be verified by a statement of truth unless the Court orders otherwise.

1425.text: A party applying for an amendment will usually be responsible for the costs of and arising from the amendment, but the Court will have regard to any failure of a party to consent to an amendment in accordance with Rule 18.12.

1429.text: 19.1 The Court may at any time order a party to:

1430.text: (1) clarify any matter which is in dispute in the proceedings; or (2) give additional information in relation to any such matter; whether or not the matter is contained or referred to in a statement of case .

1432.text: Rule 19.1 is subject to any rule of law to the contrary.

1434.text: Where the Court makes an order under Rule 19.1, the party against whom it is made must:

1435.text: (1) file his response; and (2) serve it on the other parties;

1436.text: within the time specified by the Court .

1437.text: Restriction On The Use Of Further Information

1439.text: The Court may direct that information provided by a party to another party (whether given voluntarily or following an order made under Rule 19.1) must not be used for any purpose except for that of the proceedings in which it is given.

1440.text: Preliminary Request For Further Information Or Clarification

1442.text: Before making an application to the Court for an order under Rule 19.1, the party seeking clarification or information should first serve on the party from whom it is sought a written request for that clarification or information (“a Request”), stating a date by which the response to the Request should be served. The date must allow the party providing clarification or further information a reasonable time to respond.

1444.text: A Request should be concise and strictly confined to matters which are reasonably necessary and proportionate to enable the first party to prepare his own case or to understand the case he has to meet.

1446.text: Requests must be made as far as possible in a single comprehensive document and not piecemeal.

1448.text:
A Request may be made by letter if the text of the Request is brief and the reply is likely to be brief;   
otherwise the Request should be made in a separate document.

1450.text: If a Request is made in a letter, the letter should, in order to distinguish it from any other that might routinely be written in the course of a case:

1451.text: (1) state that it contains a Request made under Part 19, and

1452.text: (2) deal with no matters other than the Request.

1454.text: A Request (whether made by letter or in a separate document) must:

1455.text: (1) be headed with the title and number of the claim;

1456.text: (2) in its heading state that it is a Request made under Part 19, identify the party seeking clarification or information and the party from whom it is sought and state the date on which it is made;

1457.text: (3) set out in a separate numbered paragraph each request for information or clarification;

1458.text: (4) where a Request relates to a document, identify that document and (if relevant) the paragraph or words to which it relates; and

1459.text: (5) state the date by which the party seeking clarification or information expects a response to the Request.

1461.text: A Request which is not in the form of a letter may, if convenient, be prepared in such a way that the response may be given on the same document:

1462.text: (1) to do this, the numbered paragraphs of the Request should appear on the left hand half of each sheet so that the paragraphs of the response may then appear on the right;

1463.text: (2) where a Request is prepared in this form an extra copy should be served for the use of the second party.
